claim: Deep agents differ from simple LLM chains in evaluation: they have state (file systems), complex outputs (multiple code changes), and require bespoke per-test-case logic rather than uniform metrics.
actionable: When evaluating agents with persistent state or file system changes, define custom success criteria per test case rather than using single metrics; write code to verify state changes, not just outputs.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: pytest integration with observability platforms automatically logs traces for each test, enabling you to link test failures to specific agent execution steps for faster debugging.
actionable: Integrate pytest with observability tools (like LangSmith) to automatically capture full traces of test execution; when a test fails, examine the exact trace to see which step failed.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: System prompts for deep agents should be 1000+ lines with concrete examples, heuristics, and role/context information, not the 3-sentence prompts used in simple chat applications.
actionable: When building autonomous agents, invest substantial effort in detailed system prompts including persona, background context, decision heuristics, and concrete examples of correct behavior in different scenarios.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: Debugging deep agents requires trace examination tools because trajectories can contain 10-100 tool calls, making visual inspection infeasible for identifying which step failed or was inefficient.
actionable: Don't attempt to debug deep agents through print statements or manual trace review; use automated observability tools that can summarize long execution traces and pinpoint specific failures.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: AI-powered trace summarization (via Poly-style assistants) allows developers to ask natural language questions about agent behavior without manually parsing complex execution traces.
actionable: Use AI assistant tools embedded in observability platforms to ask questions like 'Did this agent call the right tools?' or 'Was this inefficient?' to get instant summaries without manual trace inspection.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: CLI tools for trace fetching (LangSmith Fetch) enable coding agents to examine production traces and suggest fixes, closing the loop between observability and agent improvement.
actionable: Integrate CLI trace-fetching tools with your coding agent so it can pull recent failed traces, analyze them, and automatically suggest prompt or tool improvements.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: Evaluating multi-step agents effectively requires breaking them into single-step assertions: run the agent on step 1, verify its output, then feed step 2 conditionally, catching failures early.
actionable: For multi-step agent workflows, write tests that verify each step independently before proceeding to the next; this prevents wasting tokens on cascading failures and makes debugging faster.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: Different agent invocation modes (single step vs. full turn vs. conversational back-and-forth) require different evaluation strategies because you're testing different aspects of behavior.
actionable: For agent testing, decide whether you're evaluating single decision-making (step-level), final output (end-to-end), or conversation quality (multi-turn), and design evals accordingly.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: Clean, reproducible test environments are critical for deep agents because state matters—you can't reuse the same environment across tests without resetting it.
actionable: For each agent test case, set up a fresh environment (clean file systems, calendar state, etc.); never assume state from one test carries over to the next.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: LLM-as-a-judge evaluations for complex scenarios can assess whether agent outputs meet qualitative success criteria when deterministic checks aren't sufficient.
actionable: For subjective success criteria (e.g., 'did the email sound polite?'), use an LLM with structured output to evaluate against your specific criteria rather than hand-coding rules.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: Online evaluation (running evals on production traces in real-time) helps detect regressions and track metrics like latency and token usage automatically without manual test setup.
actionable: Set up online evals that run automatically on production agent traces to monitor latency, token usage, context fullness, and custom success metrics without requiring dedicated test infrastructure.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: Parallel execution of sub-agents (e.g., researching two topics simultaneously) speeds up complex workflows without sequential bottlenecks.
actionable: Design agents with sub-agent spawning to parallelize independent tasks; collect results when both complete rather than waiting for sequential execution.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: Context isolation through sub-agents prevents bloating the main agent's context with intermediate results (e.g., web search outputs), allowing cleaner final responses.
actionable: Delegate research, fact-checking, or other information-gathering tasks to sub-agents that return clean summaries rather than raw results, keeping main agent context focused.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain

claim: Agents became practically viable around 6-9 months ago; before that they were overhyped but non-functional, now platforms like Claude Code and Deep Research demonstrate real autonomous capability.
actionable: If you've tried agents before 2024 and found them ineffective, revisit them now; significant capability improvements in recent models have made agents practical for real workflows.
source_title: Observing & Evaluating Deep Agents Webinar with LangChain
